nodes.label: parabolic dynamics
nodes.description: The study of dynamical systems with polynomial (sub-exponential) divergence of orbits. Sits between elliptic and hyperbolic dynamics; complexity shifts into renormalisation rather than phase-space expansion.
nodes.metaphor: Like a river that meanders rather than cascades — motion exists but without the dramatic divergence of turbulence.

nodes.label: random matrix products
nodes.description: Products of matrices drawn from a probability distribution. Central objects in the study of Lyapunov exponents and multiplicative ergodic theory.
nodes.metaphor: Like repeatedly multiplying distorted maps of space — small distortions compound into something statistically predictable.

nodes.label: multifractal analysis
nodes.description: The study of the dimensional structure of level sets of ergodic averages. Quantifies the heterogeneity of fractal measures; an open question asks whether analogous formalism applies to Rauzy-type systems.
nodes.metaphor: Like measuring a coastline at every possible scale simultaneously, and asking how the roughness changes.

nodes.label: stopping times
nodes.description: Random variables representing the time at which a stochastic process satisfies a given condition. Fundamental in probability theory and ergodic theory.
nodes.metaphor: Like asking when a wandering person first steps into a particular room — a random clock tied to the path itself.

nodes.label: Lyapunov exponents
nodes.description: Quantities measuring the average exponential rate of divergence of nearby trajectories. The bridge between random matrix products and dynamical behaviour; governed by the Kontsevich–Zorich cocycle in Teichmüller dynamics.
nodes.metaphor: Like measuring how fast two nearly-identical weather forecasts diverge — the rate at which small errors become big ones.

nodes.label: spectrum as compressed geometry
nodes.description: Spectral data acts as a compressed encoding of geometric and dynamical structure, preserving some invariants but losing full detail. Highlighted by Kac's question and wave propagation in billiard domains.
nodes.metaphor: Like a musical chord that encodes the shape of an instrument — you can hear something about the geometry without seeing it.

nodes.label: dynamics vs renormalisation duality
nodes.description: When dynamics lacks expansion (parabolic systems), complexity shifts from phase space into renormalisation or parameter space. Seen in comparison between polygonal billiards, Rauzy induction, and hyperbolic systems.
nodes.metaphor: Like a sculpture that looks simple from far away but reveals intricate structure only in the tooling process, not the final form.

nodes.label: infinite renormalisation as irreducibility
nodes.description: Infinite renormalisability signals that a system cannot be reduced to a finite description and retains structure across all scales. Evidenced by Rauzy induction, continued fractions, and the Rauzy gasket.
nodes.metaphor: Like a fractal that always reveals new structure when you zoom in — it refuses to resolve into something simpler.

nodes.label: wave–particle dual encoding
nodes.description: Linear wave phenomena encode underlying nonlinear trajectory dynamics, with periodic orbits leaving measurable spectral signatures. Formalised by trace formulas in billiard and quantum contexts.
nodes.metaphor: Like hearing the drumbeat of footsteps through a wall — the wave carries information about the discrete thing that created it.

nodes.label: parameter-space fractals replace phase-space fractals
nodes.description: In non-expanding systems, fractal structure emerges in parameter space via renormalisation rather than in phase space. Contrasts Rauzy gasket (parameter) with Cantor repellers (phase space).
nodes.metaphor: Like finding that the map of possible routes is more intricate than any single journey — the complexity lives in the choice space, not the path.

nodes.label: spectral geometry
nodes.description: Study of how geometric properties of a domain are encoded in the eigenvalues of its Laplacian. Foundational question: can one hear the shape of a drum?
nodes.metaphor: Like asking whether you can reconstruct a drum from its sound alone — using frequencies to infer shape.

nodes.label: Laplacian spectrum
nodes.description: The set of eigenvalues of the Laplace–Beltrami operator on a domain, governing wave and heat behaviour. The central object of spectral geometry.
nodes.metaphor: Like the natural frequencies of a shape — the tones it would ring at if struck.

nodes.label: billiard flow
nodes.description: Hamiltonian dynamical system of a particle reflecting off the boundary of a domain. Periodic billiard trajectories contribute to spectral trace formulas.
nodes.metaphor: Like a snooker ball on a frictionless table with perfectly elastic walls, studied over infinite time.

nodes.label: trace formula
nodes.description: A relation expressing spectral data (eigenvalue sums) in terms of periodic orbits of the underlying classical system. Bridges quantum and classical descriptions.
nodes.metaphor: Like a dictionary between two languages — each entry on the spectral side corresponds to an entry on the orbit side.

nodes.label: polygonal billiards
nodes.description: Billiard flow in a polygon, leading to parabolic dynamics with singularities at corners. Connects to translation surfaces via the unfolding construction.
nodes.metaphor: Like billiards on a table with straight walls but awkward corners — the corners create the difficulty.

nodes.label: translation surface
nodes.description: A flat surface with cone-angle singularities obtained by unfolding a rational billiard. The natural geometric object for studying IETs and Teichmüller dynamics.
nodes.metaphor: Like unfolding a mirror-maze into a flat surface by keeping track of where each reflection glues to.

nodes.label: interval exchange transformation (IET)
nodes.description: A piecewise isometry of an interval encoding first return maps of directional flows on translation surfaces. Generalises circle rotations.
nodes.metaphor: Like cutting a ribbon into pieces and rearranging them in a fixed order — a piecewise rigid shuffle.

nodes.label: Rauzy induction
nodes.description: A renormalisation procedure for interval exchange transformations, analogous to the Euclidean algorithm. Acts as a discrete Poincaré section of the Teichmüller flow.
nodes.metaphor: Like the Euclidean algorithm for rhythms — repeatedly subtracting the smaller from the larger to reveal the irreducible pattern.

nodes.label: Teichmüller flow
nodes.description: Continuous deformation of translation surfaces via directional stretching. Drives the dynamics on moduli spaces of translation surfaces.
nodes.metaphor: Like stretching a rubber sheet in one direction while compressing in another, and watching how the geometry deforms.

nodes.label: Rauzy gasket
nodes.description: A fractal subset of the IET parameter space defined by infinite renormalisability under Rauzy induction. Analogue of a Sierpiński-type fractal in this setting.
nodes.metaphor: Like the Cantor set but for three-interval shuffles — the set of all patterns that never simplify under renormalisation.

nodes.label: continued fractions / Gauss map
nodes.description: Renormalisation scheme for circle rotations, providing the 1D model for infinite renormalisation. The Gauss map is the corresponding dynamical system on the parameter space.
nodes.metaphor: Like peeling an onion of approximations — each step reveals how well a number is approximated by rationals at that scale.

nodes.label: Kontsevich–Zorich cocycle
nodes.description: A cocycle over the Teichmüller flow describing growth rates of relative cohomology. Its Lyapunov exponents govern deviation phenomena in translation surface dynamics.
nodes.metaphor: Like tracking how a vector rotates and stretches as you follow the Teichmüller flow — a bookkeeping device for deviation.

nodes.label: substitution tilings
nodes.description: Aperiodic tilings generated by inflation rules, producing fractal structures via repeated scaling. Canonical example: Penrose tilings.
nodes.metaphor: Like a sentence generated by a grammar that replaces each word with a fixed phrase — the pattern repeats at every scale.

nodes.label: multifractality in Rauzy-type systems?
nodes.description: Is there a multifractal formalism or spectrum associated to Rauzy induction or the Rauzy gasket, analogous to hyperbolic systems? Would unify parabolic renormalisation with thermodynamic formalism.

nodes.label: spectral interpretation of polygonal billiards?
nodes.description: How precisely do orbit family degeneracies in polygonal billiards affect spectral statistics and trace formulas? Clarifies limits of wave–particle correspondence in non-hyperbolic settings.

nodes.label: translation surfaces and aperiodic order?
nodes.description: Under what conditions do translation surfaces derived from tilings exhibit genuine aperiodic order in their dynamics? Connects billiards, tilings, and quasicrystal-type structures.

nodes.label: bounded-type analogues in Rauzy dynamics?
nodes.description: What is the precise analogue of bounded continued fractions in Rauzy induction, and how does it control dynamical deviation? Would link quantitative ergodic properties to the irrational analogy.

nodes.label: Kac (1966)
nodes.description: Foundational paper framing the inverse spectral problem: do Laplacian eigenvalues determine a domain up to isometry? ('Can one hear the shape of a drum?')

nodes.label: Gordon–Webb–Wolpert (1992)
nodes.description: Constructed isospectral but non-isometric domains, answering Kac's question negatively.

nodes.label: Hermann Weyl
nodes.description: Established the asymptotic relationship between the eigenvalue counting function and domain volume (Weyl's law).

nodes.label: Masur–Veech theory
nodes.description: Describes ergodicity and invariant measures for IETs and translation surfaces; foundational for the modern theory.

nodes.label: Gutzwiller trace formula
nodes.description: Connects quantum spectral data with classical periodic orbits; the foundational tool for wave–particle dual encoding.

edges.explanation: Lyapunov exponents are the primary quantities computed from random matrix products via the Furstenberg–Kesten theorem.
edges.sentence: Random matrix products give rise to Lyapunov exponents via the Furstenberg–Kesten theorem, which governs their almost-sure growth rate.

edges.explanation: Vanishing or zero Lyapunov exponents characterise the parabolic regime; understanding them is central to parabolic dynamics.
edges.sentence: Lyapunov exponents are a key tool for parabolic dynamics, since their vanishing or near-vanishing characterises the parabolic regime.

edges.explanation: Multifractal spectra of Lyapunov exponents describe the fine structure of hyperbolic and partially hyperbolic systems.
edges.sentence: Multifractal analysis is a tool for studying Lyapunov exponents, describing the dimensional structure of their level sets.

edges.explanation: Return time statistics and inducing schemes use stopping times to convert parabolic systems into more tractable hyperbolic ones.
edges.sentence: Stopping times are used in parabolic dynamics via inducing schemes, converting slow systems into faster hyperbolic ones.

edges.explanation: Both encode complex structure via compressed representations — spectral frequencies versus renormalisation sequences.
edges.sentence: Spectrum as compressed geometry is analogous to Rauzy induction — both encode complex structure into a compressed representation, one via frequencies and one via renormalisation sequences.

edges.explanation: Periodic billiard trajectories determine spectral features of a domain via trace formulas.
edges.sentence: Billiard flow is a tool for spectral geometry, since periodic orbits of the flow determine the domain's spectral features via trace formulas.

edges.explanation: Directional flows on polygons correspond to IETs via first return maps to transversals.
edges.sentence: Polygonal billiards are analogous to interval exchange transformations, connected via first return maps of directional flows.

edges.explanation: Rauzy induction acts as a discrete Poincaré section of the continuous Teichmüller flow on moduli space.
edges.sentence: Rauzy induction is a tool for studying the Teichmüller flow, acting as its discrete Poincaré section on moduli space.

edges.explanation: The Rauzy gasket is the canonical example of a parameter-space fractal arising from infinite renormalisation.
edges.sentence: The Rauzy gasket is a concrete instance of the intuition that parameter-space fractals replace phase-space fractals in parabolic systems.

edges.explanation: Continued fractions / the Gauss map provide the 1D model for infinite renormalisation; Rauzy induction is the higher-dimensional generalisation.
edges.sentence: Continued fractions are a special case of Rauzy induction, providing the one-dimensional model whose higher-dimensional generalisation Rauzy induction constitutes.

edges.explanation: Both produce fractal structures via repeated scaling rules — substitution tilings in physical space, the Rauzy gasket in parameter space.
edges.sentence: Substitution tilings unexpectedly relate to the Rauzy gasket — both produce fractal structure through repeated scaling, one in physical space and one in parameter space.

edges.explanation: Infinite structure resists finite spectral encoding, highlighting fundamental limits of spectral compression as a description of complex dynamics.
edges.sentence: Infinite renormalisation as irreducibility is in tension with spectrum as compressed geometry — infinite multi-scale structure resists finite spectral encoding.

edges.explanation: Trace formulas are the mathematical formalisation of wave–particle dual encoding: they express spectral sums in terms of periodic orbits.
edges.sentence: Wave–particle dual encoding is the intuition formalised by trace formulas, which express spectral sums in terms of classical periodic orbits.

edges.explanation: The Gutzwiller trace formula is the canonical result instantiating wave–particle correspondence for quantum systems.
edges.sentence: The Gutzwiller trace formula is a context for the trace formula concept, providing the canonical quantum-classical instance of wave–particle correspondence.

edges.explanation: Kac's 1966 paper is the foundational framing of the inverse spectral problem that defines spectral geometry as a field.
edges.sentence: Kac's 1966 paper is the foundational context for spectral geometry, posing the inverse spectral problem that defines the field.

edges.explanation: Gordon–Webb–Wolpert resolved Kac's question negatively, defining a key boundary of spectral geometry.
edges.sentence: Gordon–Webb–Wolpert (1992) is a key result in spectral geometry, resolving Kac's question negatively by constructing isospectral non-isometric domains.

edges.explanation: Masur–Veech theory provides the ergodic theory of IETs and translation surfaces, including existence of invariant measures.
edges.sentence: Masur–Veech theory is the foundational context for interval exchange transformations, establishing ergodicity and the existence of invariant measures.

edges.explanation: Polygonal billiards are a primary example of parabolic dynamics — polynomial divergence arising from corner singularities.
edges.sentence: Polygonal billiards are a primary example of parabolic dynamics, exhibiting polynomial orbit divergence from corner singularities.

edges.explanation: Translation surfaces arise from unfolding rational billiards, converting reflection dynamics into linear flows.
edges.sentence: Translation surfaces are a tool for studying polygonal billiards, arising from the unfolding construction that converts reflection dynamics into linear flows.

edges.explanation: IETs encode the first return maps of directional flows on translation surfaces; they are the combinatorial face of the geometric object.
edges.sentence: Interval exchange transformations are a tool for translation surfaces, encoding their directional flows as combinatorial first return maps.

edges.explanation: The intuition directly characterises what makes parabolic dynamics hard: the action is in renormalisation space, not phase space. (inferred)
edges.sentence: The intuition of dynamics vs renormalisation duality is a context for parabolic dynamics — it explains why the complexity of parabolic systems lives in renormalisation space rather than phase space.

edges.explanation: An open question asks whether Rauzy induction admits a multifractal formalism analogous to hyperbolic systems — a potential deep connection. (inferred)
edges.sentence: Rauzy induction unexpectedly relates to multifractal analysis — an open question asks whether a multifractal formalism analogous to hyperbolic systems exists for Rauzy-type dynamics. (inferred)

edges.explanation: The Lyapunov exponents of the Kontsevich–Zorich cocycle govern deviation phenomena in translation surface dynamics. (inferred)
edges.sentence: Lyapunov exponents are a context for the Kontsevich–Zorich cocycle — the cocycle's Lyapunov spectrum governs deviation phenomena in translation surface dynamics. (inferred)

edges.explanation: The question asks whether multifractal analysis extends to Rauzy-type systems.
edges.sentence: The question about multifractality in Rauzy-type systems directly concerns multifractal analysis and whether its formalism extends beyond the hyperbolic setting.

edges.explanation: The question targets Rauzy induction as the system for which a multifractal formalism is sought.
edges.sentence: The question about multifractality in Rauzy-type systems targets Rauzy induction as the system for which a multifractal thermodynamic formalism is sought.

edges.explanation: The question concerns spectral statistics of polygonal billiards specifically.
edges.sentence: The question about spectral interpretation of polygonal billiards concerns how orbit degeneracies specific to polygonal billiards affect trace formula predictions.

edges.explanation: The question asks how orbit degeneracies in polygonal billiards affect trace formula predictions.
edges.sentence: The question about spectral interpretation of polygonal billiards asks how orbit degeneracies affect the trace formula in non-hyperbolic settings.

edges.explanation: The question concerns which translation surfaces exhibit aperiodic order.
edges.sentence: The question about translation surfaces and aperiodic order asks which translation surfaces exhibit genuine quasicrystal-type dynamics.

edges.explanation: The question connects translation surfaces derived from tiling constructions to aperiodic order.
edges.sentence: The question about translation surfaces and aperiodic order connects translation surfaces derived from substitution tilings to aperiodic order.

edges.explanation: The question seeks the Rauzy analogue of bounded continued fractions.
edges.sentence: The question about bounded-type analogues in Rauzy dynamics asks for the Rauzy analogue of bounded continued fractions.

edges.explanation: Bounded continued fractions are the 1D model whose Rauzy analogue the question seeks.
edges.sentence: The question about bounded-type analogues in Rauzy dynamics seeks to generalise the theory of bounded continued fractions to the Rauzy setting.

edges.explanation: The Laplacian spectrum is the central object of spectral geometry.
edges.sentence: The Laplacian spectrum is the central object of spectral geometry — it is the collection of eigenvalues whose structure encodes geometric properties of the domain.

edges.explanation: The intuition is directly instantiated by the Laplacian spectrum as a compressed encoding of domain geometry.
edges.sentence: The intuition that spectrum acts as compressed geometry is directly instantiated by the Laplacian spectrum, which preserves some geometric invariants while losing others.
